policy_statement: Implement national green-belt / 'Green Wall' afforestation projects (e.g., plant 315,000 trees to form a 420 km wall and establish green belts of 50–180 km length and 5–20 km width) to reduce dust and adapt to desertification.
verbatim_text: These projects are the 5-year duration and started in 2015 (KUNA, 2015). Meanwhile, Kuwait has implemented a project called 'Kuwait Green Wall Project. This project aims to increase the protected areas by planting 315,000 trees for 420 Km wall along the borderline to hold back the moving sand.
has_quantifiable_target: Yes — planting 315,000 trees for a 420 Km wall; green belt dimensions 50-180 km length and 5-20 km width for other green belt projects.
has_timeline: Yes — projects started in 2015 and described as having a 5-year duration.
has_binding_mechanism: No — project/initiative (implemented by government/NGOs); not presented as legislation.
has_spatial_specificity: Yes — along the borderline / national green-belt locations (borderline, green belts in Wafra/Abdali regions referenced elsewhere).
extraction_rationale: Named, quantified national afforestation / dust-control project with specific planting target and spatial extent. This is a concrete intervention and resource commitment (planting 315,000 trees over 420 km) and includes timeline (started 2015, five-year duration).
weak_signals: "project", "aims to", "initiative", lack of explicit law/mandate, no baseline/monitoring mentioned
strong_signals: "315,000 trees", "420 Km wall", green-belt dimensions "50–180 km" length "5–20 km" width, "5-year duration", start year "2015", "plant"
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration (afforestation/green belts) → ↓ exposure to dust and desertification / ↑ resilience to land degradation
policy_mechanisms: Afforestation/large-scale tree planting; establishment of green belts/shelterbelts; vegetation-based sand stabilization and land restoration; expansion of protected/vegetated areas
edge_case_notes:
- Potential ambiguity: large-scale tree planting can also act as mitigation via carbon sequestration; however mitigation is not the stated objective here.  
- Risk/context matters: if species choice, water use, or planting methods are maladaptive (non-native, high water demand), the adaptation benefit could be undermined—policy text does not specify these details.  
- If the project relied primarily on engineered structures with incidental planting, classification might shift toward Adaptation (infrastructure) rather than NbS.

policy_statement: Expand national desalination capacity through construction and commissioning of multiple large desalination plants (examples: Al-Zour North 250 MGD (EUR 320M), Al-Zour South 145 MGD, Al Khiran 125 MGD, Doha 60 MGD) and tender further projects totalling ~285 MGD planned as of 2019.
verbatim_text: Al-Zour North IWPP (capacity: 250  Million of Gallons per Day (MGD) for EUR  320  million),  Al-Zour South Plant (capacity: 145 MGD), Al Khiran IWPP (capacity: 125 MGD), and Doha desalination plant (60 MGD) are examples of these projects. While, 4 more projects (Al-Nwaiseeb, New Shuaiba, New Doha, Doha RO phase 2) with an additional capacity of around 285 MGD are planned to be tendered as of 2019.
has_quantifiable_target: Yes — plant capacities listed (e.g., Al-Zour North IWPP 250 MGD; Al-Zour South 145 MGD; Al Khiran 125 MGD; Doha 60 MGD; plus ~285 MGD additional planned capacity). Also budget example EUR 320 million for Al-Zour North.
has_timeline: Yes — indicates planned tendering 'as of 2019' for additional projects; earlier capacity increase reported between 2000 and 2015.
has_binding_mechanism: No — infrastructure projects/ investments by ministries/agencies (administrative commitments, not cited as law in quote).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes — specific plants / locations (Al-Zour, Al Khiran, Doha, Al-Nwaiseeb, New Shuaiba, New Doha).
extraction_rationale: Government investment and planned expansion of desalination capacity listed with named plants, capacities and an investment value for one plant. This is a specific infrastructure intervention with numeric capacities and at least one budget figure — qualifies as resource allocation and specific intervention.
weak_signals: planned, planned to be tendered, examples, are planned, planned as of 2019, tender
strong_signals: 250 MGD, 145 MGD, 125 MGD, 60 MGD, ~285 MGD planned, EUR 320M, named plants (Al-Zour North/South, Al Khiran, Doha, Al-Nwaiseeb, New Shuaiba, New Doha), construction and commissioning, tender
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (water scarcity/drought) → INCREASED adaptive capacity (improved water supply/reduced exposure to water shortages)
policy_mechanisms: Construction and commissioning of large-scale desalination plants (IWPPs)
reverse osmosis/thermal desalination via independent water and power projects)
co_benefits: economic/infrastructure development and job creation; potential increased energy demand and associated GHG emissions (negative co‑effect)
edge_case_notes:
- The verbatim text does not explicitly state climate-driven rationale; desalination could be driven by population/economic growth rather than climate impacts. If the primary driver were non-climatic resource demand, classification might be Resource Provision (but under provided taxonomy, still adaptation-like). 
- Desalination is energy-intensive and, if powered by fossil-based electricity, could increase GHG emissions; if paired with low-carbon power, it could have mitigation co-benefits (potential dual-label). IWPPs (integrated water & power projects) may blur boundaries between adaptation and energy policy.
- Not an NbS because it relies on engineered processes; not Resource Efficiency because it increases supply rather than reducing input per unit of service.

policy_statement: Establish and operate eMISK: an electronic national environmental monitoring and GIS database (eMISK) to compile and analyze geo-environmental data to support adaptation planning and monitoring.
verbatim_text: Kuwait Environmental Public Authority (EPA) has established an electronic environmental Monitoring Information System of Kuwait (eMISK) and beatona. eMISK is an electronic system established by the Environment Public Authority (EPA) of Kuwait and aims at building and maintaining a comprehensive geo-environmental database of Kuwait and a GIS system to facilitate updating and analyzing environmental data. The project starts in 2015 by EPA and is still ongoing.
has_quantifiable_target: No numeric target in the quoted text (project scope described qualitatively).
has_timeline: Yes — project starts in 2015 and is described as ongoing.
has_binding_mechanism: No — administrative project implemented by EPA (not presented as law).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes — national environmental geospatial database covering Kuwait.
extraction_rationale: Named national environmental information system established by EPA with an implementation start-year and ongoing status. This is a concrete institutional/technical intervention (data system) to support adaptation and monitoring — meets 'specific intervention' criterion.
weak_signals: No numeric targets; "ongoing" (no deadline); administrative project (no legal mandate)
strong_signals: "2015" (start date); "national"/"Kuwait" (spatial specificity); "electronic system"/"GIS"/"database"/"environmental monitoring" (concrete technical action); "establish and operate" (actionable verbs)
policy_mechanisms: National environmental monitoring system; centralized GIS database; compilation and analysis of geo-environmental data; information platform for planning and monitoring
co_benefits: Improved environmental governance and decision-making; potential to inform mitigation (emissions monitoring/inventory)
edge_case_notes: None significant — the only potential ambiguity is that environmental monitoring systems can also support mitigation (e.g., emissions inventories) or resource management; however the verbatim text explicitly states the system's role in "adaptation planning and monitoring," making Adaptation clearly primary.

policy_statement: Apply national regulation to prohibit outdoor work between 11:00 and 16:00 during June–August to reduce heat-related health risks.
verbatim_text: Adjust the official working hours Initiative Source :  the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labor of Kuwait issued a national law in 2005 to stop any outdoor work from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM during the months of June through August. The EPA started this project in 2018 under the Public Health Sector.
has_quantifiable_target: Yes — temporal restriction on outdoor work: stop outdoor work from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM during June through August.
has_timeline: Yes — law enacted 2005 and EPA project referenced 2018; the rule applies June–August annually.
has_binding_mechanism: Yes — described as a national law issued by the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labor (2005).
has_spatial_specificity: Yes — national (applies to Kuwait according to the text).
extraction_rationale: National occupational/regulatory measure restricting outdoor work hours during hottest months to reduce heat exposure — presented as a national law with clear hours and months. This is a binding labour/health regulation (legal or regulatory instrument).
weak_signals: reduce (aspirational outcome language), project, lacks explicit enforcement/penalties, no baseline/health-target metric, unspecified exemptions
strong_signals: 11:00–16:00 (hours), June–August (months), national law (2005), prohibit/stop outdoor work, national scope (Kuwait), annual temporal restriction
policy_mechanisms: National regulation / legal ban on outdoor work during specified hours; adjustment of official working hours; occupational heat-safety regulation; seasonal work-time restrictions
edge_case_notes:
- The policy is framed as occupational/public health regulation rather than explicitly as a climate policy; nonetheless it addresses a climate hazard (extreme heat).  
- If the policy were primarily justified or designed for productivity or labor-law reasons unrelated to heat risk, classification might be ambiguous, but the text cites heat-related health risks so Adaptation is appropriate.
